lists.title: Day pack
lists.summary: For a single day on the trail in the Valley or the high country. Spring through fall.

items.name: 20–25L pack with a hip belt
items.note: Hip belt matters more than the brand. It should sit on your iliac crest, not your waist.

items.name: Paper map of the park
items.note: Cell service dies past Crane Flat. A physical map does not need a signal.

items.name: Baseplate compass
items.note: Paired with the paper map, it is useful when the phone is dead or lost.

items.name: Downloaded offline maps
items.note: Gaia or Maps.me. Download the tile set at home before you leave; the park has no reliable data connection.

items.name: Garmin inReach Mini or similar satellite messenger
items.note: Two-way texting and SOS when there is no cell signal, which is most of the high country.

items.name: Power bank, 10,000 mAh
items.note: Backup for phone navigation and the satellite messenger. Charge it the night before.

items.name: Wide-brim sun hat
items.note: Granite reflects. A baseball cap is not enough above 7,000 feet.

items.name: Long-sleeve sun shirt, UPF 50
items.note: Light color, hood if you can find it. Wear it even in heat.

items.name: Sunscreen, SPF 50, reef-safe
items.note: Reapply every two hours at elevation.

items.name: Polarized sunglasses, UV400
items.note: Polarized lenses cut glare off granite and water.

items.name: SPF lip balm
items.note: Lips chap and burn faster than skin at elevation.

items.name: Patagonia Nano Puff, or any packable insulated jacket
items.note: The Valley is warm at 10am and 40°F at the rim by 3pm. Synthetic insulation still works if it gets wet.

items.name: Packable rain shell with taped seams
items.note: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer high country.

items.name: Lightweight gloves
items.note: Worth carrying for Tuolumne.

items.name: Extra wool or synthetic socks
items.note: Wet socks cause blisters. A dry pair costs almost no weight.

items.name: Headlamp plus spare battery
items.note: Day hikes turn into night hikes more often than you would think.

items.name: Small first aid kit
items.note: Blister care is what you actually use.

items.name: Leukotape or moleskin
items.note: Apply before hot spots form.

items.name: Lighter or waterproof matches
items.note: Emergency fire only. Campfires are banned above 9,600 feet.

items.name: 2L reservoir plus 1L bottle
items.note: Reservoir for steady sipping, bottle for filtering refills. Both, not either.

items.name: Squeeze water filter
items.note: The Sawyer kind. Cheap, fast, and keeps working when the temperature drops.

items.name: Trail snacks, twice what you think
items.note: Calories matter more than weight up here. Bring real food: bars, nuts, jerky, dried fruit, hard candy.

items.name: Over-the-head mosquito head net
items.note: Meadows and creek crossings in spring and early summer can be dense with mosquitoes.

items.name: Trowel, TP, and Ziploc
items.note: For catholes on longer day hikes.

items.name: Trekking poles
items.note: They reduce knee stress on steep descents.

items.name: Small dry bag for phone and electronics
Cash in small bills
items.note: Parking is cash-only at several trailheads.

items.name: Hiking boots or trail runners
items.note: Road sneakers do not grip wet granite.

lists.title: Overnight backpack
lists.summary: For one to four nights in the backcountry. This adds to the day pack list, it does not replace it.

items.name: 55 to 65L pack
items.note: Bigger than you need is worse than tighter than you want.

items.name: Three-season tent
items.note: Freestanding, two doors, under three pounds if you can afford it.

items.name: Extra tent stakes
items.note: Longer stakes hold in sandy, rocky soil.

items.name: 20°F sleeping bag
items.note: A 30°F bag is not enough above 8,000 feet.

items.name: Inflatable pad, R-value 4 or higher
items.note: Cold ground steals heat all night.

items.name: Sleeping bag liner
items.note: It adds 5 to 15°F of warmth.

items.name: Stove plus 4oz canister
items.note: Figure one canister per two days, per person. Bring one extra.

items.name: Lightweight cookpot
items.note: 750ml for one, 1.3L for two.

items.name: Lighter plus backup
items.note: Carry two. They vanish and they fail.

items.name: Biodegradable camp soap
items.note: Wash dishes 200 feet from water.

items.name: Bear canister, required
items.note: Yosemite rents Garcia jugs at the wilderness center cheaply but they are heavy. Upgrading to a BearVault 500, or a 450 for shorter trips, is worth it. All food, trash, and scented items go inside, and store it 50 feet or more from your tent.

items.name: Wilderness permit, required year-round
items.note: Carry a physical or digital copy. It specifies your trailhead and entry date, and it covers the use of a backpacking stove in the park.

items.name: Camp shoes
items.note: Cheap foam sandals. Worth their weight every single night.

items.name: Camping shovel, the poop shovel
items.note: You know what this is for. Practice Leave No Trace: dig a 6-to-8-inch cathole 200 feet from water and trail, do your business, bury it. Pack a double-lined Ziploc to carry your TP back out. Or, if you want to be a real outdoor junkie, just use rocks. No, seriously. You can just use rocks.

items.name: Toothbrush and small toothpaste
items.note: Spit 200 feet from water.

items.name: Power bank with cables
items.note: There is no cell service in the backcountry.

items.name: Satellite messenger or PLB
items.note: A Garmin inReach or SPOT works. A PLB needs no subscription.

items.name: Extra headlamp batteries or spare headlamp
Mosquito head net
items.note: June through mid-August, the lakes and meadows are brutal.

lists.title: Car trip
lists.summary: The full trunk load for any Yosemite drive. Pack it once, leave it packed.

items.name: The John box
items.note: Named after my friend John, who came up with the idea. The John box is a single durable storage box that holds every camping essential you ever bring: a double-burner Coleman stove, propane, a hatchet, paracord, firestarter, a flashlight, a spare headlamp and batteries, a lantern, a tarp, even a deck of playing cards. Mine doubles as a camp chair and a small table. The whole point is that you don't unpack it between trips. You load it once, store it loaded, and grab it on the way out the door. You'll never forget the propane again. If you'd rather skip the build, John sells a premade version with everything you need to survive and thrive in the wild.

items.name: Tent sized for the group plus one
Extra tent stakes
items.note: Rocky Sierra soil bends cheap ones.

items.name: Sleeping bag, one per person
items.note: Rate it to at least 20°F.

items.name: Extra blankets
items.note: One wool blanket per tent.

items.name: Extra tarp
items.note: Stage it separately so you always have a spare.

items.name: Pop-up shade canopy
items.note: Eight hours of August sun makes this mandatory.

items.name: Stove windscreen
items.note: Sierra afternoons are windy.

items.name: Spare standard 1 lb propane canister
items.note: Always carry one extra.

items.name: Little Kamper refillable 1 lb propane
items.note: A DOT-certified refillable canister sold at the Village Store and other in-park locations on an exchange model. Buy one, then swap empties for full ones. The park disposes of roughly 24,000 single-use cylinders left at campsites every year, so this is the responsible alternative.

items.name: 1 lb-to-bulk-tank adapter hose
items.note: Cheaper fuel on longer trips.

items.name: Portable grilling grate
items.note: Campsite grates vary.

items.name: Thin flexible cutting board
items.note: It rolls flat and rinses in seconds.

items.name: Eating utensils, one set per person
Spice kit
items.note: Salt, pepper, garlic, paprika, red pepper, olive oil.

items.name: Cooking oil in a squeeze bottle
Collapsible wash basin
items.note: A two-basin wash and rinse uses less water.

items.name: 5 gallon jug with a spigot
items.note: Not for drinking. For radiators, hand-washing, the unexpected.

items.name: Water filter
items.note: The Sawyer kind, as a backup if the spigot is closed.

items.name: Firewood, bought in-park
items.note: California invasive-pest rules apply and rangers enforce them at checkpoints. Buy it locally.

items.name: Small metal shovel or trowel
items.note: For spreading coals and smothering the fire.

items.name: Metal water bucket
items.note: Full extinguishment is required before you leave a fire.

items.name: Valley campfire hours awareness
items.note: Campfires in Valley campgrounds are restricted to certain evening hours, and high-country wilderness fires are banned above 9,600 feet. Propane stoves stay legal during fire bans.

items.name: Folding saw
items.note: Cuts what the hatchet bounces off.

items.name: Additional paracord or bank line, 100 feet
Small toolkit
items.note: Screwdrivers, a wrench, pliers.

items.name: Cable locks
items.note: Secure gear to the rack or table.

items.name: Headlamp, one per person
items.note: Vault toilets at midnight are not the place to share.

items.name: Power bank, 20,000 mAh
items.note: Cell service is unreliable past Crane Flat.

items.name: Standard folding camp chairs, one per person
items.note: You will use them more than anything else you bring.

items.name: Packable backpacking-style camp chairs, one or two extra
items.note: Folds to the size of a water bottle and goes in the John box.

items.name: Small folding camp table
items.note: Keeps the picnic table from becoming a staging area.

items.name: Tote bags for grocery and bear-locker runs
Comprehensive first aid kit
items.note: Blister treatment, moleskin, a SAM splint, an ace bandage, antiseptic, gauze, tape, and OTC meds. Note that bear spray is not permitted in Yosemite. Do not bring it.

items.name: Lithium jump pack
items.note: Better than cables. Cell service is unreliable past Crane Flat.

items.name: Tire chains, November through April
items.note: Required during chain controls and rangers check. Practice once at home.

items.name: Basic car kit
items.note: Spare, jack, lug wrench.

items.name: Tick-check reminder
items.note: Yosemite has Lyme-carrying ticks.

items.name: Biodegradable camp soap
items.note: Keep it 200 feet from water.

items.name: Toiletries kit
items.note: Toothbrush, paste, floss, deodorant, lip balm, feminine items. All in the bear locker overnight.

items.name: Shower sandals or flip-flops
items.note: Curry Village and Housekeeping showers require them.

items.name: Quarters and small bills
items.note: For the coin showers.

items.name: Grey-water container
items.note: Do not dump dishwater on the ground.

items.name: Cooler with ice
items.note: Bear-aware: nothing with a scent stays in the car overnight.

items.name: Block ice
items.note: It lasts longer. Freeze it in a cleaned jug.

items.name: Coffee setup
items.note: Pour-over or percolator, grounds, filters, a manual grinder.

items.name: Snack bag
items.note: Granola, trail mix, jerky, dried fruit. There is one grocery store in the Valley and the line is long.

items.name: Small supply of paper plates and cups
Packable rain jacket
items.note: Summer afternoon thunderstorms are not rare.

items.name: Extra socks, two pairs per day
Swimwear
items.note: The Merced swimming holes are real, cold, and worth it.

items.name: Printed campsite and permit confirmation
items.note: Include your Half Dome confirmation if you have one.

items.name: Bear-locker discipline
items.note: All food and scented items, including deodorant, toothpaste, chapstick, sunscreen, and trash, go in the bear box. The trunk is not bear-proof.

items.name: Park entrance receipt or America the Beautiful pass
items.note: Keep it on the dashboard.
